llms.txt

Assets

Theme assets — CSS, JavaScript, fonts, and static images — live in the assets/ subdirectory of your theme and are referenced with absolute paths in your templates.

Where assets live

Every theme has an assets/ directory at its root. This is where you put all static files that belong to the theme's visual design: stylesheets, scripts, fonts, icons, and any static images used by the theme itself (such as logos and background textures).

outpost/themes/my-theme/
  assets/
    style.css
    main.js
    fonts/
      inter-regular.woff2
      inter-bold.woff2
    icons/
      arrow.svg
      close.svg
    images/
      logo.svg
      og-default.jpg

There is no asset pipeline. Files in the assets/ directory are served exactly as-is — no bundling, no minification, no content hashing. The web server delivers them directly as static files, bypassing PHP entirely. This keeps the setup simple and deployment fast.

Referencing assets in templates

Reference theme assets using absolute paths. The path structure is always /outpost/themes/{theme-slug}/assets/{filename}:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css">
<script src="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/main.js"></script>
<img src="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/images/logo.svg" alt="Logo">

Always use absolute paths (starting with /). Relative paths will break on any URL that is not the root — for example, a relative path in head.html works on / but fails on /about or /post/hello-world. Absolute paths work everywhere.

Warning — The theme slug in asset paths must match the directory name exactly, including capitalization. /outpost/themes/My-Theme/ and /outpost/themes/my-theme/ are different paths on case-sensitive file systems (Linux). Always use lowercase directory names to avoid this problem.

No asset pipeline — intentional simplicity

Outpost deliberately avoids a build step for theme assets. There is no webpack, no Vite, no PostCSS, no Sass compilation. What you put in assets/ is what gets served.

This is a deliberate design choice. Outpost themes are meant to be editable by developers who are not Node.js engineers. A designer working on a client site should be able to open style.css, change a color, upload the file, and see the result immediately — without running a build command.

If you prefer to use a build tool for development (Vite, esbuild, Sass), you can absolutely do so — just configure its output to write to assets/. The final deployed files in assets/ are what matter.

Cache busting

Because static files are served directly, browsers cache them aggressively. When you update style.css, visitors who have the old version cached will not see your changes until their cache expires.

The simplest solution is to add a version query string to your asset URLs. The browser treats a URL with a different query string as a different resource and fetches fresh:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css?v=2">

Increment the version number each time you deploy changes to that file. You can also use a date or build number:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css?v=20260304">

This manual approach is low-tech but reliable. It requires remembering to bump the version in your template whenever you change the file — typically in partials/head.html for CSS and partials/footer.html for JS.

Tip — If you are using a build tool, configure it to include a content hash in the output filename (e.g., style.a3f9b1.css) and update the reference in your partial each build. This is the most reliable form of cache busting because the URL changes only when the content changes.

Recommended directory structure

There are no required subdirectory names within assets/, but this structure is used by official Outpost themes and serves most projects well:

Directory / FilePurpose
style.cssMain stylesheet — all site styles
main.jsMain JavaScript file
fonts/Self-hosted web font files (.woff2, .woff)
icons/SVG icon files referenced in templates
images/Static theme images — logo, placeholder images, decorative backgrounds

You are not required to follow this structure. Organize your assets however makes sense for your theme. The web server will serve any file in the theme directory as long as the path is correct.

Fonts

There are two approaches to web fonts in Outpost themes:

Google Fonts (or any CDN)

Link to the font in partials/head.html. This is the fastest approach for development — no files to manage.

<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">

The downside: visitors make an external request to Google's servers, which has minor privacy implications and a small latency cost. For production sites where font performance matters, self-hosting is better.

Self-hosted fonts

Download the font files (in .woff2 format, which has excellent browser support) and place them in assets/fonts/. Then declare them in your stylesheet:

/* In assets/style.css */
@font-face {
  font-family: 'Inter';
  src: url('/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/fonts/inter-regular.woff2') format('woff2');
  font-weight: 400;
  font-style: normal;
  font-display: swap;
}

@font-face {
  font-family: 'Inter';
  src: url('/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/fonts/inter-bold.woff2') format('woff2');
  font-weight: 700;
  font-style: normal;
  font-display: swap;
}

Use font-display: swap to prevent invisible text while the font loads. The woff2 format is supported by all modern browsers — you do not need .woff or .ttf fallbacks for new sites.

User-uploaded images vs. theme images

There is an important distinction between two kinds of images in Outpost:

TypeLocationHow to use
Theme static images outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/images/ <img src="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/images/logo.svg">
User-uploaded images outpost/uploads/ {{ hero_image | image }} in template

Theme images are part of the theme's visual design — the logo, decorative backgrounds, placeholder images. They ship with the theme and do not change when a site editor uploads new content.

User-uploaded images are managed through the Media Library and entered into content fields via the admin editor. They live in outpost/uploads/, not in the theme directory. Access them in templates using the | image filter: {{ featured_image | image }}. This filter outputs the full URL to the uploaded file.

Note — Never store user-uploaded content in the theme's assets/ directory. The uploads directory (outpost/uploads/) persists across theme switches and deployments. The theme's assets directory may be overwritten when a theme is updated.

SVG icons in templates

Small SVG icons are often inlined directly in HTML for performance and to allow CSS styling. For larger SVG files like logos, the <img> tag is cleaner. Either approach works in Outpost templates — template tags inside SVG attributes are processed normally:

<!-- External SVG via img tag -->
<img src="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/icons/arrow.svg" alt="">

<!-- Inline SVG (paste SVG code directly) -->
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24">
  <path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"/>
</svg>
